nodes.text: Capitalization Policy
nodes.section_path: Capitalization Policy

nodes.text: Aurora Retail Holdings
nodes.section_path: Aurora Retail Holdings

nodes.text: Effective Date: January 1, 2024
nodes.section_path: Effective Date: January 1, 2024

nodes.text: Owner: Corporate Finance – Controller’s Office
nodes.section_path: Owner: Corporate Finance – Controller’s Office

nodes.text: 1. Purpose
nodes.section_path: 1. Purpose

nodes.text: 1.1 The purpose of this Capitalization Policy is to establish consistent criteria for determining when expenditures are recorded as capital assets versus current period expenses.
nodes.section_path: 1.1 The purpose of this Capitalization Policy is to establish consistent criteria for determining when expenditures are recorded as capital assets versus current period expenses.

nodes.text: 1.2 A clear and consistently applied capitalization policy improves the quality and comparability of Aurora Retail Holdings’ financial statements and supports compliance with applicable accounting standards (e.g., US GAAP or IFRS, as adopted by the Company).
nodes.section_path: 1.2 A clear and consistently applied capitalization policy improves the quality and comparability of Aurora Retail Holdings’ financial statements and supports compliance with applicable accounting standards (e.g., US GAAP or IFRS, as adopted by the Company).

nodes.text: 1.3 This policy is intended for use by Finance, Accounting, Procurement, and any team that initiates or approves spend related to property, equipment, software, and other long-lived assets.
nodes.section_path: 1.3 This policy is intended for use by Finance, Accounting, Procurement, and any team that initiates or approves spend related to property, equipment, software, and other long-lived assets.

nodes.text: 2. Scope
nodes.section_path: 2. Scope

nodes.text: 2.1 This policy applies to all entities and business units consolidated into Aurora Retail Holdings’ financial statements.
nodes.section_path: 2.1 This policy applies to all entities and business units consolidated into Aurora Retail Holdings’ financial statements.

nodes.text: 2.2 The policy covers tangible fixed assets (e.g., leasehold improvements, fixtures, IT hardware, warehouse equipment) and intangible assets (e.g., internally developed software, purchased software licenses with multi‑year benefit).
nodes.section_path: 2.2 The policy covers tangible fixed assets (e.g., leasehold improvements, fixtures, IT hardware, warehouse equipment) and intangible assets (e.g., internally developed software, purchased software licenses with multi‑year benefit).

nodes.text: 2.3 This policy does not apply to:
nodes.section_path: 2.3 This policy does not apply to:

nodes.text: a) Inventory
nodes.section_path: a) Inventory

nodes.text: b) Prepaid expenses with a benefit period shorter than 12 months
nodes.section_path: b) Prepaid expenses with a benefit period shorter than 12 months

nodes.text: c) Research and development activities that do not meet capitalization criteria under the applicable accounting framework
nodes.section_path: b) Prepaid expenses with a benefit period shorter than 12 months

nodes.text: 3. Capitalization Thresholds
nodes.section_path: 3. Capitalization Thresholds

nodes.text: 3.1 Individual tangible assets
nodes.section_path: 3.1 Individual tangible assets

nodes.text: 3.1.1 Tangible assets are capitalized when:
nodes.section_path: 3.1.1 Tangible assets are capitalized when:

nodes.text: a) The total cost of the asset or project is greater than or equal to USD 5,000; and
nodes.section_path: 3.1.1 Tangible assets are capitalized when:

nodes.text: b) The asset is expected to provide economic benefit for more than 12 months.
nodes.section_path: 3.1.1 Tangible assets are capitalized when:

nodes.text: 3.1.2 Tangible assets with a cost below USD 5,000 are normally expensed in the period incurred, unless they are part of a larger project that meets the threshold at the project level.
nodes.section_path: 3.1.2 Tangible assets with a cost below USD 5,000 are normally expensed in the period incurred, unless they are part of a larger project that meets the threshold at the project level.

nodes.text: 3.2 Asset groups and projects
nodes.section_path: 3.2 Asset groups and projects

nodes.text: 3.2.1 Related items that form part of a single project (e.g., store remodel, warehouse automation upgrade) should be evaluated together.
nodes.section_path: 3.2.1 Related items that form part of a single project (e.g., store remodel, warehouse automation upgrade) should be evaluated together.

nodes.text: 3.2.2 If the total project cost is greater than or equal to USD 25,000 and the project extends the useful life, capacity, or functionality of an existing asset, the project may be capitalized even if individual components fall below USD 5,000.
nodes.section_path: 3.2.2 If the total project cost is greater than or equal to USD 25,000 and the project extends the useful life, capacity, or functionality of an existing asset, the project may be capitalized even if individual components fall below USD 5,000.

nodes.text: 3.3 Intangible assets and software
nodes.section_path: 3.3 Intangible assets and software

nodes.text: 3.3.1 Internally developed software and implementation costs are capitalized when:
nodes.section_path: 3.3.1 Internally developed software and implementation costs are capitalized when:

nodes.text: a) The project passes preliminary feasibility (approved business case and design); and
nodes.section_path: 3.3.1 Internally developed software and implementation costs are capitalized when:

nodes.text: b) The software is expected to be used for more than 12 months; and
nodes.section_path: 3.3.1 Internally developed software and implementation costs are capitalized when:

nodes.text: c) The total project cost is greater than or equal to USD 50,000.
nodes.section_path: 3.3.1 Internally developed software and implementation costs are capitalized when:

nodes.text: 3.3.2 Purchased software licenses are capitalized when:
nodes.section_path: 3.3.2 Purchased software licenses are capitalized when:

nodes.text: a) The license term exceeds 12 months; and
nodes.section_path: 3.3.2 Purchased software licenses are capitalized when:

nodes.text: b) The upfront payment is greater than or equal to USD 10,000.
nodes.section_path: 3.3.2 Purchased software licenses are capitalized when:

nodes.text: 4. Capitalization Method and Procedures
nodes.section_path: 4. Capitalization Method and Procedures

nodes.text: 4.1 Initial measurement
nodes.section_path: 4.1 Initial measurement

nodes.text: 4.1.1 Capitalized cost includes:
nodes.section_path: 4.1.1 Capitalized cost includes:

nodes.text: a) Purchase price (net of discounts and rebates)
nodes.section_path: a) Purchase price (net of discounts and rebates)

nodes.text: b) Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to the location and condition necessary for its intended use (e.g., installation, configuration, freight)
nodes.section_path: a) Purchase price (net of discounts and rebates)

nodes.text: c) Non‑refundable taxes and duties
nodes.section_path: c) Non‑refundable taxes and duties

nodes.text: 4.1.2 Costs that are not directly attributable (e.g., general overhead, training) are expensed as incurred unless specifically allowed under applicable accounting standards.
nodes.section_path: 4.1.2 Costs that are not directly attributable (e.g., general overhead, training) are expensed as incurred unless specifically allowed under applicable accounting standards.

nodes.text: 4.2 Subsequent expenditures
nodes.section_path: 4.2 Subsequent expenditures

nodes.text: 4.2.1 Expenditures that extend the useful life, increase capacity, or improve functionality beyond the original specification may be capitalized as part of the existing asset.
nodes.section_path: 4.2.1 Expenditures that extend the useful life, increase capacity, or improve functionality beyond the original specification may be capitalized as part of the existing asset.

nodes.text: 4.2.2 Routine maintenance, repairs, and minor enhancements that do not meet the above criteria are expensed in the period incurred.
nodes.section_path: 4.2.2 Routine maintenance, repairs, and minor enhancements that do not meet the above criteria are expensed in the period incurred.

nodes.text: 4.3 Capital project workflow
nodes.section_path: 4.3 Capital project workflow

nodes.text: 4.3.1 All capital projects must be initiated via an approved capital expenditure (CapEx) request, including:
nodes.section_path: 4.3.1 All capital projects must be initiated via an approved capital expenditure (CapEx) request, including:

nodes.text: a) Project description
nodes.section_path: a) Project description

nodes.text: b) Estimated total cost
nodes.section_path: a) Project description

nodes.text: c) Business justification and expected benefits
nodes.section_path: a) Project description

nodes.text: d) Estimated in‑service date
nodes.section_path: a) Project description

nodes.text: 4.3.2 The Controller’s Office is responsible for reviewing CapEx requests for compliance with this policy before project codes are created.
nodes.section_path: 4.3.2 The Controller’s Office is responsible for reviewing CapEx requests for compliance with this policy before project codes are created.

nodes.text: 4.3.3 Upon completion, the project owner must confirm:
nodes.section_path: 4.3.3 Upon completion, the project owner must confirm:

nodes.text: a) Actual cost incurred
nodes.section_path: 4.3.3 Upon completion, the project owner must confirm:

nodes.text: b) Assets placed in service date
nodes.section_path: 4.3.3 Upon completion, the project owner must confirm:

nodes.text: c) Any unused budget to be released
nodes.section_path: 4.3.3 Upon completion, the project owner must confirm:

nodes.text: 5. Depreciation and Amortization Guidelines
nodes.section_path: 5. Depreciation and Amortization Guidelines

nodes.text: 5.1 Useful lives
nodes.section_path: 5.1 Useful lives

nodes.text: 5.1.1 Typical useful lives for major asset classes are:
nodes.section_path: 5.1.1 Typical useful lives for major asset classes are:

nodes.text: a) Leasehold improvements: 5–10 years or lease term, whichever is shorter
nodes.section_path: 5.1.1 Typical useful lives for major asset classes are:

nodes.text: b) Store fixtures and equipment: 3–7 years
nodes.section_path: b) Store fixtures and equipment: 3–7 years

nodes.text: c) IT hardware: 3–5 years
nodes.section_path: c) IT hardware: 3–5 years

nodes.text: d) Internally developed software: 3–5 years
nodes.section_path: c) IT hardware: 3–5 years

nodes.text: 5.1.2 Deviations from standard useful lives must be documented and approved by the Controller’s Office.
nodes.section_path: 5.1.2 Deviations from standard useful lives must be documented and approved by the Controller’s Office.

nodes.text: 5.2 Depreciation basis and method
nodes.section_path: 5.2 Depreciation basis and method

nodes.text: 5.2.1 Depreciation is calculated on a straight‑line basis over the asset’s estimated useful life, net of estimated residual value.
nodes.section_path: 5.2.1 Depreciation is calculated on a straight‑line basis over the asset’s estimated useful life, net of estimated residual value.

nodes.text: 5.2.2 Depreciation begins when the asset is placed in service (available for its intended use), not when the project is approved or cash is paid.
nodes.section_path: 5.2.2 Depreciation begins when the asset is placed in service (available for its intended use), not when the project is approved or cash is paid.

nodes.text: 5.3 Impairment and disposals
nodes.section_path: 5.3 Impairment and disposals

nodes.text: 5.3.1 Assets are reviewed for indicators of impairment at least annually and when events or changes in circumstances suggest that carrying values may not be recoverable.
nodes.section_path: 5.3.1 Assets are reviewed for indicators of impairment at least annually and when events or changes in circumstances suggest that carrying values may not be recoverable.

nodes.text: 5.3.2 Gains or losses on disposal are recognized in the period of disposal as the difference between proceeds and net book value.
nodes.section_path: 5.3.2 Gains or losses on disposal are recognized in the period of disposal as the difference between proceeds and net book value.

nodes.text: 6. Recordkeeping and Controls
nodes.section_path: 6. Recordkeeping and Controls

nodes.text: 6.1 Fixed asset register
nodes.section_path: 6.1 Fixed asset register

nodes.text: 6.1.1 All capitalized assets must be recorded in the fixed asset register with:
nodes.section_path: 6.1.1 All capitalized assets must be recorded in the fixed asset register with:

nodes.text: a) Asset ID
nodes.section_path: a) Asset ID

nodes.text: b) Description
nodes.section_path: b) Description

nodes.text: c) Location
nodes.section_path: c) Location

nodes.text: d) In‑service date
nodes.section_path: d) In‑service date

nodes.text: e) Cost and accumulated depreciation
nodes.section_path: d) In‑service date

nodes.text: f) Responsible department
nodes.section_path: f) Responsible department

nodes.text: 6.1.2 The fixed asset register is maintained by the Corporate Accounting team.
nodes.section_path: 6.1.2 The fixed asset register is maintained by the Corporate Accounting team.

nodes.text: 6.2 Documentation
nodes.section_path: 6.2 Documentation

nodes.text: 6.2.1 Supporting documentation (invoices, contracts, CapEx approvals, project completion certificates) must be retained for at least seven (7) years or longer where required by local regulation.
nodes.section_path: 6.2.1 Supporting documentation (invoices, contracts, CapEx approvals, project completion certificates) must be retained for at least seven (7) years or longer where required by local regulation.

nodes.text: 6.2.2 For software projects, documentation should include project charters, design approvals, and evidence of transition from preliminary to development stage.
nodes.section_path: 6.2.2 For software projects, documentation should include project charters, design approvals, and evidence of transition from preliminary to development stage.

nodes.text: 6.3 Policy exceptions
nodes.section_path: 6.3 Policy exceptions

nodes.text: 6.3.1 Exceptions to this policy require written approval from the Controller and the VP of Finance.
nodes.section_path: 6.3.1 Exceptions to this policy require written approval from the Controller and the VP of Finance.

nodes.text: 6.3.2 Approved exceptions must be clearly documented and retained with the associated asset file.
nodes.section_path: 6.3.2 Approved exceptions must be clearly documented and retained with the associated asset file.

nodes.text: 7. Review and Updates
nodes.section_path: 7. Review and Updates

nodes.text: 7.1 This policy will be reviewed at least every two years by the Controller’s Office.
nodes.section_path: 7.1 This policy will be reviewed at least every two years by the Controller’s Office.

nodes.text: 7.2 Changes to capitalization thresholds or useful lives must be approved by the CFO.
nodes.section_path: 7.2 Changes to capitalization thresholds or useful lives must be approved by the CFO.
